primaryAngle: Premium materials and detailed workmanship for Clayton's architectural diversity

dominantEras: 1900s–1940s — original Old Town Clayton brick and frame homes

neighborhoods.name: Old Town Clayton
neighborhoods.profile: Pre-WWII brick and frame homes near the city's commercial core; architecturally diverse blocks of Tudor, Colonial Revival, and four-square stock.

neighborhoods.name: Hi-Pointe / Skinker DeBaliviere edge
neighborhoods.profile: Eastern Clayton blocks adjoining city limits; older homes plus mid-century apartments and condos.

neighborhoods.name: DeMun
neighborhoods.profile: Walkable urban village with small-lot single-family homes and architecturally significant rowhouses near Forest Park.

neighborhoods.name: Wydown / Forsyth corridor
neighborhoods.profile: Larger-lot luxury homes along Wydown and Forsyth Boulevards; mix of preserved historic estates and new luxury construction.

commonRoofingChallenges: Architectural diversity means material recommendations must match each home individually — there's no Clayton 'standard'
Detail-oriented homeowners who notice everything; cut corners on flashing or color match show up immediately
Tight residential streets requiring careful dumpster placement and crew logistics
Mature tree canopy increasing branch-strike and debris-clearing scope
uniqueAngleElaboration: Clayton is unusual in St. Louis County for how architecturally diverse its housing stock is on a single block. You can stand on Wydown Boulevard and see a 1920s Tudor Revival next to a 1960s mid-century modern next to a 2018 contemporary teardown — and a roofing recommendation that's right for one is wrong for the next two. That diversity, combined with the Clayton homeowner's tendency to notice every detail, means premium-quality materials and meticulous workmanship are non-negotiable. Designer asphalt, slate, copper accents, and tile are all common across Clayton's better blocks; cheap 3-tab work would be visible from across the street. Logistics also matter — Clayton's tight residential streets and mature canopy mean dumpster placement and crew flow have to be planned carefully, and tree-strike repairs are a regular part of post-storm work in established neighborhoods.
sourcesConsulted: General knowledge of St. Louis County housing stock and architectural distribution
